title: Diwali & Navratri
page_texts:
As Diwali approached, Sita’s small village in India felt alive.
Homes were being cleaned, Lanterns were being hung.

Everyone seemed excited.

Sita felt it too.

*

__ The next afternoon, Sita ran outside to join h

They were helping decorate the lane witl

_ Some held lanterns, Some untangled strings.

rt

’ Sita tied careful knots and smiled

As evening approached, Sita gently poured colored powder

onto the earth. Her friends followed, shaping patterns side by side.
Each swirl and petal felt special.

Soon, Sita and her friends gathered small pieces of clay.

They pressed, shaped, and smoothed them carefully.

Tiny bowls began to form in their hands.
“These will hold the light,” Sita said softly.

“After shaping diyas, Sita’s hands were dusty with clay. ~
She hurried inside and washed them with cool water. —

: The sink splashed and the soap bubbles floated.

1 “Now I'm ready for the next part,” Sita said, smiling.

4

Sita stepped outside again and breathed in deeply.

The sweet smell of laddoos drifted through the evening air.
Lights shimmered softly around her.
She paused.

Why do we celebrate Diwali?.
_ What is the story behind all these light:

‘Sita came and sat beside her grandmother.

Her grandmother began telling a story—
a story about hope and sadness,
about darkness and brightness,

and how happiness returned to the world aga

Sita listened carefully.

Sita stepped outside once more.
» Her friends stood beside her.

The lanterns glowed. -
The diyas shimmered, —

The rangoli sparkled wi

shone brightly.

Sita smiled.

As Sita walked back home,

she noticed one doorway still dark.

She quietly placed another diya there
and lit it gently. :

She smiled.

_ Even one small light

could make someone's night brighter.

The morning after Diwali, the village felt softer.
The bright fireworks were gone, but a peaceful feeling remained.

Sita stepped outside and saw faint rangoli colors still glowing

in the sun.

Now it was time for something different.

Navaratri was about to begin.

Sita and her friends gathered again.
This time, they were not hang:

They were helping decorate small prayer places.

They brought flowers from the garden.

They folded colored cloth neatly on a table.
One friend brought a tiny clay pot.

» The decorations felt calm and beautiful.

THES ‘ a

4

Another day a and her grandmother prepared for prayer.

They brought flowers and neat cloth.
They set a tiny clay pot on the altar.
Sita’s grandmother placed a small deity—
a god or goddess—to be the center.

“Maybe she will help us feel peaceful,” Sita’s grandmother

said.

When everything was ready,
"d

On the first days, they prayed to Durga.

Sita’s grandmother told Sita a small part of her story—

how Durga faced a powerful demon who caused fear everywhere:
She did not run aw

She stood brave and strong.

“You do not need to fight demons,”

her grandmother smiled,

“but you must stand ip for what is right.

but about harmony and happiness.

“Where there is kindness and gratitude,”
her grandmother said gently,

“Lakshmi’s grace is there.”

That evening, Sita helped her friends

share fruit and sweets with others.

“Maybe this is our Lakshmi,”

Sita whispered with a smile.

Another day, Sita placed her school books near the altar of Saraswati.
Her grandmother told her a small story—

that Saraswati sits by a river of knowledge,

holding music and learning in her hands.

“She reminds us that learning is sacred,”

her grandmother said softly,

Sita gently touched her notebooks

and promised to always try her best. ,

On the evenings of Navaratri, the village came alive again,
but differently than Diwali.

Music played softly at first, then louder.

Sita and her friends picked up colorful sticks

_and formed circles for Dandiya Raas.

‘Tap, tap, turn, smile.

os. Y

That night, Sita sat quietly.. ~ oe nee
_ Diwali had been about bright outer light.

In a small village in India, a girl named Sita helps prepare for the
bright festival of Diwali and the peaceful nights of Navaratri.

As she decorates with friends, listens to her grandmother's stories,
and learns about courage, kindness, and wisdom,

Sita begins to understand something important.

Light does not only shine from lamps.

Sometimes, it shines from within.

Coming Soon: Sita Celebrating Holi

title: Holi
page_texts:
Hi, I’m Sita.

I came to India

with my
grandmother.

~
Come join us!

Hi, Aji! I came to come
celebrate Holi with you.

“Hello, my dear!
Come inside.”

KE

Aji told me the story of Holi.

“Long ago, there was an evil king who tried to
use his magical powers to hurt people.
But he could not harm the prince
Prahlad because Prahlad’s heart
was good and full of love.

To celebrate the victory of good

over evil, we throw colors and

_ laughter into the air!”

The next morning, Sita went to the
market with her grandmother —_

to buy colors and white
_ clothes for Holi.

ag

The next morning, Sita jumped out of bed.
It was Holi!

She raced downstairs.

“Happy Holi!” Ajj laughed.

“Come, let’s get the colors ready outside!”

2 ERT ipe

$e.
ees.

i OE”

a Sita ran outside in her bright white clothes.

“Happy Holi!” she laughed,

throwing colors into the air.

Splish! Splash! Whoosh!

The world burst into rainbows.

The children played Holi all afternoon.
When they were tired, they came inside
and dusted the colors off.

“Story time!” Aji called.
The children gathered around to hear
how people celebrate Holi all across India.

After story time, the children changed

into their bright, colorful Indian clothes.

In the kitchen, Aji handed Sita a cool white drink.
“This is called Thandai,” Aji said.

“People drink it on Holi.”

Then she placed a half-moon shaped

sweet into Sita’s hand.

“These are called Gujiya,” Aji smiled,
They are crunchy and sweet—like

__ little dumplings filled with happin

y

Sita and Aji carried a plate of Thandai and Gujiya
to their neighbor’s house.

The neighbor opened the door with a

bright smile and warm hugs.

She brought out another plate of

Thandai and Gujiyatoshare. __

Thad so much fun celebrating Holi today. _

Holi is one of my favorite festivals.

I can’t wait to see what my next adventure
will be.

Until then... namaste.

~ About the Authos :

Kiran is an imaginative young writer who loves

festivals, bright colors, and happy stories.

She created Sita Celebrating Holi to celebrate

family, traditions, and fun.

This is just the beginning of Sita’s adventures!

Join Sita as she travels to India to celebrate —

- Holi with her grandmother.

From colorful markets to sweet treats
and joyful splashes of color, Sita discovers
that Holi is more than a festival— :
it’s a celebration of love,

family, and togetherness.

Come celebrate with Sita!

She ran down the stairs. Aji smiled and
“Today is Ganesha’s birthday!” 5am

In the kitchen, Aji was cooking
something called modaks.
“These are Ganesha’s favorite “S

sweets,” she explained.

oe

n the glowing prayer room, Aji shared a story. a

“Once, after eating many sweets,

Ganesha’s round tummy was very full.”

When the moon laughed at him, o
Ganesha taught it an important ~~

lesson about kindness and respect.” a

Later, Sita and Aji took plates of sweets

cool drinks to their neighbors. Everyone

As the sun began to set, everyone gathered for

se together in song as the lamps

t fill with joy.
As the night grew quiet, the lamps still flickered

About the Author

Kieran is 8 years old.

She loves learning about Indian festivals

and celebrating them. She also enjoys

writing stories to share about them.

Celebrate Ganesh Chaturthi

with Sita and her grandmother!

Experience the joy of Ganesha's
birthday through Sita’s eyes,

‘ a filled with sweets, songs,
a un and festive traditions.
